enrichment.short_description: Concourse CI is a free, open-source, container-based automation system for CI/CD, built around declarative YAML pipelines, resources, and jobs.
enrichment.seo_title: Concourse CI: Open-Source CI/CD Pipeline Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Concourse CI is a free, open-source, container-based CI/CD automation tool with declarative YAML pipelines. Self-hosted, written in Go under Apache 2.0.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Concourse CI is a free, open-source, container-based automation system used primarily for continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD). It was created in 2014 by Alex Suraci and Chris Brown, two engineers on the Pivotal Cloud Foundry team who grew frustrated with the plugin-heavy, click-configured nature of Jenkins while running Cloud Foundry's own build pipelines. The first commit dates to April 13, 2014, and the project was released as open source in 2015 with sponsorship from Pivotal. Concourse is licensed under Apache 2.0 and has no proprietary or paid edition; it must be self-hosted, as there is no official managed SaaS offering. The project is written mostly in Go (the web/ATC component) with Elm powering its browser UI, and as of mid-2026 its GitHub repository (concourse/concourse) has roughly 7,900 stars, with the latest release (v8.2.4) shipped in June 2026. Concourse is opinionated about a small set of principles: idempotency, immutability, declarative configuration, stateless workers, and reproducible builds. Rather than a UI built from checkboxes, pipelines are defined as a single declarative YAML file, functioning like what the project describes as 'a distributed, continuous Makefile.' Every pipeline is built from three primitives: resources (versioned external inputs/outputs such as Git repos or Docker images), tasks (units of work executed inside isolated containers), and jobs (sequences of tasks triggered by resource changes). Because every task runs in its own container with an explicitly specified image, builds are isolated and reproducible across machines. Concourse deliberately avoids a traditional plugin architecture; instead, integrations with external systems (Git, S3, Docker registries, Slack, and so on) are implemented as 'resource types' — ordinary container images conforming to a simple interface — so the core system stays small while still being extensible to virtually any tool. A companion CLI called fly lets developers execute a pipeline task locally with uncommitted changes before pushing, which shortens the debug loop compared to CI systems that only run on already-pushed code. The web dashboard renders pipelines as a graph so failures can be traced visually from a job back to its root task. Concourse can be deployed as a standalone binary, via Docker Compose for local development, through a Kubernetes Helm chart, or via a BOSH release for larger production deployments, with PostgreSQL as its backing datastore and separate web and worker nodes for scaling. The project has been supported over the years by organizations including the Cloud Foundry Foundation, Pix4D, SAP, and RamNode, and it maintains its own public pipelines at ci.concourse-ci.org as a real-world example of the tool in use. Concourse is best suited for engineering teams that want infrastructure-as-code style, git-versioned pipelines with strong reproducibility guarantees, and that are comfortable running and operating their own CI infrastructure rather than relying on a hosted SaaS CI product such as GitHub Actions or CircleCI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Concourse CI is a free, open-source automation system focused on CI/CD, originally built in 2014 by engineers on Pivotal's Cloud Foundry team who were dissatisfied with Jenkins' plugin-driven, UI-configured pipelines. It was open sourced in 2015 and remains fully community- and sponsor-supported, with no paid tier or official hosted service.

The project treats pipelines as first-class, version-controlled artifacts defined in declarative YAML rather than assembled through a web UI. Every build step runs inside an isolated container, giving Concourse strong guarantees around reproducibility and idempotency that distinguish it from more traditional, stateful CI servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pipelines are built from three primitives: resources (versioned inputs and outputs like Git repositories or container images), tasks (containerized units of work), and jobs (sequences of tasks triggered by resource changes). This model lets Concourse express complex, multi-stage delivery workflows without a plugin marketplace — new integrations are just container images implementing a simple resource interface.

The fly CLI lets developers run a task locally with uncommitted changes before pushing, shortening the feedback loop, while the web UI visualizes pipelines as a graph for tracing failures back to their source. Concourse can be installed as a standalone binary, via Docker Compose, through a Kubernetes Helm chart, or as a BOSH release, with PostgreSQL as the backing database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Concourse CI is entirely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license — there are no paid plans, seat limits, or usage caps imposed by the project itself. The only real cost is the infrastructure a team provisions to run the web, worker, and PostgreSQL nodes.

Because there is no official managed SaaS offering, organizations that don't want to operate their own Concourse deployment typically look to hosted CI alternatives instead; Concourse is aimed squarely at teams willing to self-host in exchange for full control over their pipeline infrastructure.

features.title: Declarative Pipelines
features.description: Pipelines are defined as version-controlled YAML rather than configured through a UI, making them reviewable and reproducible like any other code.

features.title: Resources, Tasks & Jobs Model
features.description: A small set of primitives — versioned resources, containerized tasks, and triggered jobs — composes into arbitrarily complex delivery workflows.

features.title: Container-Based Execution
features.description: Every task runs in its own container with an explicit image, isolating dependencies and ensuring builds behave the same across machines.

features.title: fly CLI for Local Testing
features.description: Developers can execute a pipeline task locally with uncommitted changes before pushing, avoiding the push-fail-fix loop common to hosted CI.

features.title: Custom Resource Types Instead of Plugins
features.description: Integrations with external systems are implemented as container images conforming to a simple interface, avoiding a heavyweight plugin ecosystem.

features.title: Visual Pipeline Dashboard
features.description: The web UI renders pipelines as a graph, letting engineers trace a failure from a single click back to its root cause.

features.title: Multiple Deployment Options
features.description: Concourse ships as a standalone binary, a Docker Compose setup, a Kubernetes Helm chart, or a BOSH release for larger deployments.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or vendor lock-in
Strong reproducibility from fully containerized, isolated task execution
Declarative, git-versioned pipelines avoid UI-configuration sprawl
Scales horizontally via stateless worker nodes
cons: Steeper learning curve than point-and-click CI tools like GitHub Actions
No official managed/SaaS hosting — teams must operate their own infrastructure
Smaller plugin/integration ecosystem than Jenkins
Self-hosting requires running and maintaining PostgreSQL plus web and worker nodes

faqs.question: Is Concourse CI free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Concourse is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no paid tiers; you only pay for the infrastructure you run it on.

faqs.question: Does Concourse offer a hosted or SaaS version?
faqs.answer: No official managed SaaS exists. Concourse is designed to be self-hosted using a standalone binary, Docker Compose, a Helm chart, or a BOSH release.

faqs.question: What language is Concourse written in?
faqs.answer: The core web component (ATC) and workers are written in Go, while the browser-based dashboard UI is built with Elm.

faqs.question: How is Concourse different from Jenkins?
faqs.answer: Concourse uses declarative, version-controlled pipeline YAML and fully containerized task execution instead of Jenkins' UI-configured jobs and plugin ecosystem.

faqs.question: Can I test a pipeline task locally before pushing?
faqs.answer: Yes, the fly CLI's execute command runs a task locally with your uncommitted changes, letting you debug before committing.

use_cases.title: CI/CD for Containerized Applications
use_cases.description: Teams building containerized services use Concourse's fully isolated task execution to get reproducible builds across environments.

use_cases.title: Custom Multi-Tool Delivery Pipelines
use_cases.description: Organizations needing to integrate many internal or uncommon tools build custom resource types instead of waiting on a plugin marketplace.

features: Standalone binary, Docker Compose, Helm chart, or BOSH release deployment
Community support via GitHub Discussions and Discord

enrichment.short_description: Concrete CMS is a free, open-source content management system with in-context page editing, version history, and granular permissions, plus optional managed hosting.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free open-source core, paid managed hosting)
enrichment.seo_title: Concrete CMS: Open-Source CMS Features & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Concrete CMS is a free, open-source CMS with in-context editing and version history. Self-host under MIT license or use managed hosting from $4.99/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Concrete CMS is a free, open-source content management system originally developed in 2003 as part of building LewisAndClark200.org, the official site for the Ad Council's National Council for the Lewis & Clark Bicentennial. That project's underlying toolkit evolved into an open-source CMS eventually known as concrete5, maintained by PortlandLabs Inc., a Portland, Oregon-based company that began in 2002 as a full-service interactive media firm under founders Franz and Andy. On July 7, 2021, concrete5 was officially rebranded to Concrete CMS, dropping the version number from the name; the rebrand coincided with the release of version 9 in October 2021, which added multisite support, a boards feature, a new gallery block, and a UI rebuilt on Bootstrap 5. The software is licensed under the MIT license, making it free to download, modify, and self-host without restriction. As of 2021, Concrete CMS reported more than 62,000 live websites built on the platform. Its defining feature is in-context editing: users can edit page content, images, and layout directly on the live page rather than through a separate back-end interface, which lowers the technical bar for non-technical content editors. The platform also provides built-in version history for every page (similar to a wiki), a fine-grained, role-based permissions system suited to organizations with complex approval workflows, a model-view-controller (MVC) architecture for developers, an embedded image editor, integrated server-side caching, a documented developer API, and native SEO tooling. A marketplace offers additional themes and add-ons, both free and paid, for extending functionality. The legacy 5.x branch (concrete5) reached end of life in August 2019 with final release 5.6.4.0; the current line is version 9.x, with 9.4.2 released in July 2025 as the latest stable build. Beyond the free, self-hosted open-source core, PortlandLabs also operates concretecms.com, offering managed hosting plans that bundle infrastructure, updates, and support; pricing for these managed plans starts around $4.99/month for a Starter tier and roughly $19/month for a Business tier, according to third-party software review aggregators, though exact current pricing should be confirmed on Concrete CMS's own site since managed hosting pricing changes more frequently than the open-source license terms. Concrete CMS is positioned as an alternative to WordPress and Drupal for organizations — particularly government, educational, and enterprise sites — that need strong built-in permissions and page-level version control without heavy reliance on third-party plugins. It supports typical CMS use cases including corporate websites, intranets, and content-heavy institutional sites where multiple contributors with different access levels need to publish and revise pages independently.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Concrete CMS traces its roots to 2003, when its core toolkit was built for LewisAndClark200.org, the Ad Council's official Lewis & Clark Bicentennial site. That toolkit grew into the open-source CMS long known as concrete5, maintained by PortlandLabs Inc. of Portland, Oregon, and formally rebranded to Concrete CMS in July 2021 alongside the version 9 release.

The software is free and open source under the MIT license, with more than 62,000 reported live sites as of 2021. Its hallmark is in-context editing — content is edited directly on the live page rather than in a separate admin panel — paired with page-level version history and a fine-grained, role-based permissions system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Concrete CMS is built on an MVC architecture with a documented developer API, integrated server-side caching, and a built-in image editor. Version 9 (October 2021) added multisite management, a boards feature, and a gallery block, all running on a Bootstrap 5-based UI; the current stable release is 9.4.2 (July 2025).

A marketplace of themes and add-ons extends the core platform, and native SEO tooling is built in rather than bolted on through third-party plugins, which is one reason the CMS has historically found traction with government and institutional sites that need granular editorial permissions out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Concrete CMS software itself is free under the MIT license with no cost to download, install, or modify. Organizations that self-host only pay for their own server infrastructure.

For teams that don't want to manage hosting themselves, PortlandLabs offers managed hosting plans through concretecms.com, with published starting prices around $4.99/month for an entry-level Starter tier and roughly $19/month for a Business tier that includes more resources and support.

features.title: In-Context Editing
features.description: Content editors can modify text, images, and layout directly on the live page instead of through a separate admin interface.

features.title: Page Version History
features.description: Every page revision is tracked, wiki-style, allowing content to be reviewed and rolled back.

features.title: Fine-Grained Permissions
features.description: A role-based permissions system supports complex organizational approval and editing workflows.

features.title: MVC Architecture & Developer API
features.description: Developers can extend and customize the platform through a documented API and standard MVC structure.

features.title: Built-In SEO Tooling
features.description: Native search-engine-optimization features are included without requiring third-party plugins.

features.title: Multisite Support
features.description: Version 9 introduced the ability to manage multiple sites from a single Concrete CMS installation.

features.title: Marketplace of Themes & Add-Ons
features.description: A marketplace offers additional free and paid themes and functionality extensions.

pros: Free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Strong built-in permissions and page version history without extra plugins
In-context editing is approachable for non-technical content editors
Active marketplace and optional managed hosting for teams that want it
cons: Smaller plugin, theme, and developer ecosystem than WordPress
Managed hosting adds recurring cost beyond the free open-source core
Sites still on the legacy 5.x (concrete5) branch require migration to stay supported
Fewer third-party integrations available compared to larger CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is Concrete CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is free and open source under the MIT license. Optional managed hosting from PortlandLabs is a paid service starting around $4.99/month.

faqs.question: Is Concrete CMS the same as concrete5?
faqs.answer: Yes. concrete5 was officially rebranded to Concrete CMS in July 2021, alongside the version 9 release.

faqs.question: Who maintains Concrete CMS?
faqs.answer: PortlandLabs Inc., a company based in Portland, Oregon, that has maintained the project since its early concrete5 days.

faqs.question: What's new in Concrete CMS 9?
faqs.answer: Multisite support, a boards feature, a gallery block, and a UI rebuilt on Bootstrap 5, released in October 2021.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Concrete CMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be downloaded and self-hosted for free under the MIT license, or run on PortlandLabs' managed hosting plans.

use_cases.title: Government and Institutional Websites
use_cases.description: Organizations needing granular, role-based editorial permissions use Concrete CMS's built-in access controls.
use_cases.audience: Public sector and institutional web teams

use_cases.title: Small Business and Agency Sites
use_cases.description: In-context editing lets non-technical staff update site content without training on a separate admin panel.

enrichment.short_description: Conduit is a lightweight, open-source Matrix homeserver written in Rust, packaged as a single binary with an embedded database for easy self-hosting.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Conduit is a free, open-source Matrix homeserver written in Rust. It runs as a single binary with an embedded database, ideal for low-resource self-hosting.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Conduit is a lightweight, open-source homeserver implementation of the Matrix protocol, the open standard for secure, decentralized, federated real-time communication. The project was started by developer Timo Kösters, with its first commit made in February 2020, and has been supported by Famedly, a Berlin-based secure-messaging company that also works on Matrix-based products for regulated industries such as healthcare. In 2021, the project received six months of funding from the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF), a public grant aimed at supporting open-source digital infrastructure. Conduit is written in Rust and distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, and its source lives on GitLab under the Famedly namespace, with a GitHub mirror also available. Its central design goal is simplicity: Conduit ships as a single binary with an embedded database (RocksDB by default), meaning operators do not need to separately install and manage PostgreSQL or another external database server, unlike Synapse, the original and most widely deployed Matrix homeserver written in Python by Matrix's core team. This makes Conduit substantially easier to set up and lighter on resources, with the project explicitly targeting hardware as modest as a Raspberry Pi. Functionally, Conduit implements the core Matrix specification needed to federate with other Matrix homeservers, meaning users on a self-hosted Conduit instance can communicate with users on Synapse, Dendrite, or any other compliant homeserver, as well as bridge to external platforms such as Discord via Matrix bridges. As of its most recent public documentation, the project describes itself as beta: usable in real deployments but still missing some smaller features relative to more mature homeservers. Notably, Conduit's development has partly diverged into community-driven forks. 'Conduwuit' emerged as a hard fork that added performance improvements and features at a faster pace than upstream Conduit, and it was later succeeded by a project called 'Tuwunel,' which describes itself as conduwuit's official successor. This means prospective self-hosters researching 'Conduit' today may also encounter these related but distinct projects, and should verify which codebase best matches their needs for feature completeness and active maintenance. Conduit itself carries no commercial pricing or company behind it as a product — it is purely open-source software that individuals or organizations run on their own infrastructure, so there is no subscription, seat pricing, or vendor lock-in, only the cost of the server it runs on. It is best suited for privacy-conscious individuals, small communities, and technically capable teams who want to operate their own sovereign, federated chat server without the operational overhead of running Synapse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Conduit is a free, open-source Matrix homeserver written in Rust, started by developer Timo Kösters in February 2020 with support from the Berlin-based secure-messaging company Famedly. It later received six months of funding from Germany's Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) to support its development as open digital infrastructure.

The project's core goal is simplicity: it ships as a single binary with an embedded database rather than requiring a separately managed database server, making it noticeably lighter to set up and run than Synapse, the reference Matrix homeserver implementation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Conduit implements the Matrix federation protocol, so a self-hosted instance can communicate with users on any other compliant Matrix homeserver, and can bridge to external chat platforms like Discord through standard Matrix bridges. Being written in Rust, it aims for strong performance and memory safety with low resource requirements, explicitly targeting hardware as modest as a Raspberry Pi.

The project is licensed under Apache 2.0 and hosted on GitLab under the Famedly namespace. Its most active fork, 'Conduwuit,' added features and fixes at a faster pace and has since been succeeded by a project called 'Tuwunel,' so researchers evaluating Conduit today should check which codebase is most actively maintained for their needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Conduit is entirely free and open source with no commercial tier, subscription, or vendor behind it as a product. There is no cost to download, modify, or run it.

The only expense involved is the infrastructure an individual or organization chooses to run it on, which — thanks to its low resource footprint — can be inexpensive compared to hosting a full Synapse deployment.

features.title: Single Binary Deployment
features.description: Conduit ships as one binary with no separate application server components to install.

features.title: Embedded Database
features.description: Uses an embedded database (RocksDB by default) instead of requiring a separately managed PostgreSQL instance.

features.title: Low Resource Requirements
features.description: Designed to run on modest hardware, including devices as small as a Raspberry Pi.

features.title: Matrix Protocol Federation
features.description: Communicates with any other Matrix-compliant homeserver and can bridge to platforms like Discord.

features.title: Rust-Based Implementation
features.description: Built in Rust for memory safety and performance relative to Python-based Synapse.

features.title: Open Source & Self-Hosted
features.description: Fully open source under Apache 2.0, giving operators complete control over their own chat server.

pros: Much simpler setup than Synapse, with no external database to manage
Efficient enough to run on low-cost, low-resource hardware
Free and open source with no vendor or subscription
Full Matrix federation compatibility with other homeservers and bridges
cons: Labeled beta and missing some smaller features found in Synapse
Smaller plugin/module ecosystem than more mature homeservers
Development has partly moved into community forks (Conduwuit, Tuwunel), which can create confusion about which project to use
Requires technical comfort with self-hosting and server administration

faqs.question: Is Conduit free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Conduit is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license; you only pay for the server it runs on.

faqs.question: What is Conduit built with?
faqs.answer: It's written in Rust and uses an embedded database (RocksDB by default) instead of a separate database server.

faqs.question: Who maintains Conduit?
faqs.answer: It was created by Timo Kösters, with support from Famedly, a Berlin-based secure-messaging company.

faqs.question: Is Conduit production-ready?
faqs.answer: The project describes itself as beta — usable today but still missing some smaller features found in more mature homeservers like Synapse.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Conduit, Conduwuit, and Tuwunel?
faqs.answer: Conduwuit is a community hard fork of Conduit that added features at a faster pace, and Tuwunel is described as its official successor.

use_cases.title: Self-Hosted Personal or Family Chat Server
use_cases.description: Privacy-focused individuals run their own Matrix homeserver instead of relying on a third-party chat provider.

use_cases.title: Lightweight Community Server on Low-Cost Hardware
use_cases.description: Small communities and organizations self-host federated chat on inexpensive infrastructure like a Raspberry Pi or small VPS.

enrichment.short_description: ConfigCat is a feature flag and remote configuration platform with flat-tier pricing, unlimited seats and users, and open-source SDKs for 10+ platforms.
enrichment.seo_title: ConfigCat: Feature Flags & Config Management Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ConfigCat is a feature flag platform with flat-tier pricing, unlimited seats and MAUs, and open-source SDKs. Free plan available; paid plans from $110/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ConfigCat is a feature flag and remote configuration management platform founded in 2018 in Budapest, Hungary, by three co-founders — Zoltán, Gergely Sinka, and Lajos Szőke — who bootstrapped the company without outside venture funding. According to the company's own account, the idea began when Zoltán, working as a product manager, kept running into a recurring problem around toggling and remotely configuring application behavior. He initially considered building a remote error-logging service but pivoted toward remote configuration and feature flags after finding fewer established competitors in that niche. The founders left their jobs to build ConfigCat full time and went through a difficult first year without paying customers before landing their first sale in 2019, a $200 purchase of the company's highest-tier plan. Early growth came largely through being listed on AlternativeTo.net as an alternative to LaunchDarkly, the market-leading feature flag platform, which helped ConfigCat attract larger customers including Microsoft and H&M; the company now reports an estimated 500-plus paying customers and holds SOC 2 Type II certification. ConfigCat's main differentiator versus competitors like LaunchDarkly and Split is its pricing model: rather than charging per seat or per monthly active user (MAU), which is standard among feature flag vendors, ConfigCat charges based on CDN configuration-download volume and network traffic, with unlimited seats, unlimited MAUs, and unlimited feature-flag reads included on every paid plan. As of the platform's published pricing, the tiers are: a Forever Free plan (10 feature flags, 2 environments, 2 products, 5 million config downloads per month, 20GB of network traffic, 7-day audit log retention, no credit card required); Pro at $110/month (100 feature flags, 3 environments and products, 25 million downloads per month, 100GB traffic, 35-day audit log retention); Smart at $325/month (unlimited feature flags, environments, segments and targeting rules across up to 10 products, 250 million downloads per month, 1TB traffic); Enterprise at $900/month (1 billion downloads per month, 4TB traffic, 2-year audit log retention, premium SLA support); and Dedicated at $4,500/month (6-billion-plus downloads per month, 24TB traffic, private cloud deployment options). ConfigCat provides open-source SDKs across a wide range of languages and platforms, including JavaScript, Python, Go, Java, .NET, iOS, Android, and Node.js, alongside a management dashboard, REST API, percentage-based rollouts, user segmentation, targeting rules, webhooks, and audit logging. It is used by engineering teams to progressively roll out features, run kill switches for risky changes, and manage remote configuration values without requiring a full application redeploy for every change.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ConfigCat is a Budapest, Hungary-based feature flag and remote configuration platform founded in 2018 by Zoltán, Gergely Sinka, and Lajos Szőke. The company was bootstrapped without venture funding, struggled through a slow first year, and grew largely through word of mouth after being listed as a LaunchDarkly alternative on AlternativeTo.net, eventually landing customers including Microsoft and H&M.
The platform lets engineering teams toggle features, run controlled rollouts, and manage remote configuration values without redeploying an application, and it holds SOC 2 Type II certification.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ConfigCat supports feature flags, percentage-based rollouts, audience segments, targeting rules, webhooks, and audit logs, all accessible through a dashboard or REST API. Its SDKs — covering JavaScript, Python, Go, Java, .NET, iOS, Android, and Node.js, among others — are open source.

The platform's defining feature is its pricing structure: unlike most feature flag competitors, ConfigCat does not charge per seat or per monthly active user, instead billing based on CDN configuration-download volume and network traffic, with unlimited team members and end users on every plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ConfigCat offers a Forever Free plan (10 feature flags, 5 million downloads per month, no card required) plus four paid tiers: Pro at $110/month, Smart at $325/month, Enterprise at $900/month, and Dedicated at $4,500/month, each scaling primarily by config-download volume, network traffic, and audit-log retention rather than by team size.

All plans, including the free tier, include unlimited seats, unlimited monthly active users, and API access, which differentiates ConfigCat's pricing model from per-seat competitors like LaunchDarkly.

features.title: Feature Flags & Targeting Rules
features.description: Toggle features and target specific user segments with rule-based conditions.

features.title: Flat-Tier, Usage-Based Pricing
features.description: Unlimited seats and monthly active users on every plan; pricing scales by config-download volume instead.

features.title: Open-Source SDKs
features.description: SDKs for JavaScript, Python, Go, Java, .NET, iOS, Android, Node.js, and more, all open source.

features.title: Percentage-Based Rollouts & Segments
features.description: Gradually roll out features to a percentage of users or defined audience segments.

features.title: Audit Logs
features.description: Track configuration and flag changes with retention windows that scale by plan.

features.title: Webhooks & REST API
features.description: Automate workflows and integrate flag changes into CI/CD or other internal systems.

features.title: SOC 2 Type II Compliance
features.description: Independently audited security controls suitable for enterprise procurement requirements.

pros: Predictable flat pricing regardless of team size or end-user volume
Generous free tier with no credit card required
Open-source SDKs across a wide range of languages and platforms
SOC 2 Type II certified
cons: Fewer advanced experimentation/analytics features than dedicated A/B testing platforms
Smaller company and ecosystem than category leader LaunchDarkly
Higher tiers can get expensive for applications with very high config-download volume
Audit log retention is limited on lower tiers (7-35 days) versus 2 years on Enterprise/Dedicated

faqs.question: Is ConfigCat free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Forever Free plan covers up to 10 feature flags and 5 million config downloads per month with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How is ConfigCat priced?
faqs.answer: By CDN configuration-download volume and network traffic rather than by seats or monthly active users — every plan includes unlimited team members and end users.

faqs.question: Who founded ConfigCat?
faqs.answer: Zoltán, Gergely Sinka, and Lajos Szőke founded ConfigCat in Budapest, Hungary, in 2018.

faqs.question: Is ConfigCat SOC 2 compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, it holds SOC 2 Type II certification.

faqs.question: What languages does ConfigCat support via SDK?
faqs.answer: JavaScript, Python, Go, Java, .NET, iOS, Android, and Node.js, among other platforms, all through open-source SDKs.

use_cases.title: Progressive Feature Rollouts
use_cases.description: Engineering teams gradually release new functionality to a percentage of users or specific segments to limit blast radius.

use_cases.title: Remote Configuration & Kill Switches
use_cases.description: Teams change application behavior or disable risky features instantly without a full redeploy.

features: Unlimited feature flags, environments, up to 10 products

enrichment.short_description: Confluence is Atlassian's team workspace and wiki for documentation, knowledge bases, and collaborative pages, tightly integrated with Jira.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 10 users); paid plans from $5.42/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: Confluence: Atlassian Wiki Pricing, Features & FAQ
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Confluence is Atlassian's team workspace for documentation and knowledge bases. Free for up to 10 users; paid plans start at $5.42/user/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Confluence is a team workspace and enterprise wiki product built by Atlassian, the Sydney, Australia-founded software company started in 2002 by Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar. Atlassian's first product was the issue-tracking tool Jira, released in 2002, and two years later, on March 25, 2004, Atlassian shipped Confluence 1.0 as an application built for enterprise knowledge management while retaining the simplicity of a wiki. At launch there was little direct competition for a purpose-built enterprise wiki, which helped Confluence establish itself as a default choice for software teams documenting alongside Jira. In 2014, Atlassian released Confluence Data Center, adding clustering and load-balanced high availability for large self-hosted deployments; Atlassian later phased out its traditional Server license entirely, pushing customers toward either Confluence Cloud (the SaaS product) or Confluence Data Center (self-hosted). More recently, Atlassian has integrated its Rovo AI features into Confluence for tasks like semantic search, page summarization, and content generation, bundled into paid plans. Functionally, Confluence organizes content into spaces and pages, supports real-time collaborative editing, whiteboards, and a large library of templates for use cases like product requirements, meeting notes, retrospectives, and internal knowledge bases, and integrates natively with Jira so issues and pages can be linked. As of 2026, Confluence Cloud pricing has four tiers: a Free plan supporting up to 10 users with 2GB of storage, basic pages and spaces, and 10 automation rule runs per month; a Standard plan at $5.42 per user per month (billed annually) supporting up to 150,000 users, 250GB of storage, Rovo AI, guest access, advanced permissions, and 100 automation runs per month with business-hours support; a Premium plan at $10.44 per user per month (billed annually) adding more automation capacity, expanded storage, and premium support; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan available only on annual contracts. For organizations that need to keep Confluence on their own infrastructure, Confluence Data Center is licensed per user tier, with published estimates putting the cost at roughly $28,000 per year for a 500-user deployment. Confluence is used broadly beyond software engineering — by HR, marketing, legal, and operations teams — as a central place to document processes, policies, and institutional knowledge, and its deep integration with the rest of Atlassian's suite (Jira, Trello, Loom, Bitbucket) is a primary reason organizations already using Atlassian tools adopt it as their default documentation platform rather than evaluating standalone wiki products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Confluence is Atlassian's team workspace and enterprise wiki product, first released as Confluence 1.0 on March 25, 2004, two years after Atlassian's founding and the launch of its first product, Jira. Atlassian was founded in Sydney, Australia, in 2002 by Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar.

Confluence organizes content into spaces and pages, supports real-time collaborative editing and whiteboards, and offers a large template library for use cases such as product requirements, meeting notes, and internal documentation, with native integration into Jira and the rest of Atlassian's product suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Confluence Data Center, released in 2014, added clustering and high availability for large self-hosted deployments, and remains the self-hosted option today after Atlassian discontinued its Server license. More recently, Atlassian has integrated Rovo AI into Confluence for semantic search, summarization, and content generation on paid plans.

Permissions and guest access allow fine-grained control over who can view or edit specific spaces and pages, while automation rules let teams trigger actions (like notifications or page updates) without manual work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Confluence Cloud offers a Free plan for up to 10 users, a Standard plan at $5.42 per user per month (billed annually), a Premium plan at $10.44 per user per month (billed annually), and custom-priced Enterprise plans available only on annual contracts.

Organizations that need to self-host can use Confluence Data Center, which is licensed per user tier and estimated at roughly $28,000 per year for a 500-user deployment, since Atlassian no longer sells the older, cheaper Server license.

features.title: Pages, Spaces & Whiteboards

features.title: Real-Time Collaborative Editing

features.description: A large set of templates covers use cases like product requirements, meeting notes, and retrospectives.

features.title: Jira Integration
features.description: Pages and issues link natively, since Confluence and Jira share the same Atlassian platform.

features.title: Rovo AI
features.description: AI-powered search, summarization, and content generation bundled into Standard and higher plans.

features.title: Granular Permissions & Guest Access
features.description: Fine-grained space and page-level permissions, including external guest access on paid plans.

features.title: Automation Rules
features.description: No-code automation for repetitive actions like notifications and page updates.

features.title: Confluence Data Center
features.description: A clustered, self-hosted deployment option for organizations that can't use the cloud SaaS product.

pros: Deep integration with Jira and the rest of the Atlassian ecosystem
Mature permissions model and large template library
Both cloud (SaaS) and self-hosted (Data Center) deployment options
Free plan available for very small teams
cons: Per-user pricing scales quickly for larger teams
Confluence Data Center licensing is expensive relative to the discontinued Server option
Page/space structure has a learning curve for non-technical users
Rovo AI features are gated behind paid plans

faqs.question: Is Confluence free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Free plan supports up to 10 users with basic pages, spaces, and 2GB of storage.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Confluence Cloud and Data Center?
faqs.answer: Cloud is Atlassian's hosted SaaS product; Data Center is a self-hosted, clustered option licensed per user tier for organizations that need on-premises control.

faqs.question: Does Confluence integrate with Jira?
faqs.answer: Yes, both products share Atlassian's platform, and pages can be linked directly to Jira issues.

faqs.question: When was Confluence released?
faqs.answer: Confluence 1.0 launched on March 25, 2004, two years after Atlassian's first product, Jira.

faqs.question: Does Confluence include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rovo AI is bundled starting on the Standard plan, offering search, summarization, and content generation.

faqs.question: Can I still buy a Confluence Server license?
faqs.answer: No, Atlassian has discontinued Server licensing; current self-hosted customers use Confluence Data Center instead.

enrichment.short_description: Conjur is CyberArk's open-source secrets management and machine-identity platform, providing policy-based access control and a REST API for DevOps.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free/Open Source (core); commercial tier via CyberArk Secrets Manager
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); commercial pricing is custom/quote-based
enrichment.headquarters: Newton, Massachusetts, USA (CyberArk; now part of Palo Alto Networks)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Conjur is CyberArk's open-source secrets management platform with policy-based access control. Free and self-hosted, with a commercial enterprise tier.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Conjur is an open-source secrets management and machine-identity platform originally acquired by security vendor CyberArk in 2017, which then open-sourced the technology in September 2017 to give DevOps teams a free way to secure secrets and machine identities used by CI/CD pipelines and cloud-native applications. The Conjur server is licensed under GNU LGPL v3.0, while its API clients and extensions are licensed under Apache 2.0, and the codebase is written primarily in Ruby. Its core concept is a policy-as-code language called MAML (Machine Authorization Markup Language), a YAML-based format used to declare the machines, users, and privileges that make up an organization's access model. On top of this policy layer, Conjur exposes a REST API for storing and retrieving secrets, managing identity lifecycles, and authorizing resource access, and it includes built-in authenticators and rotators that support dynamic, automatic credential rotation rather than static, long-lived secrets. Secrets are encrypted using AES-256-GCM via CyberArk's Slosilo library, and the platform supports multi-tenant accounts so multiple isolated environments can be managed from one Conjur deployment. Conjur integrates with common DevOps toolchains including Kubernetes, Docker, Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Terraform, and Jenkins, as well as major cloud infrastructure platforms, which is central to its positioning as infrastructure for securing non-human, machine-to-machine authentication at scale. The open-source edition (Conjur OSS) remains actively maintained, with its GitHub repository (cyberark/conjur) showing roughly 941 stars, 147 forks, and a latest release of v1.27.0 in June 2026. CyberArk also sells a commercial product line, formerly branded 'Conjur Enterprise' and now marketed as CyberArk Secrets Manager, which builds on the open-source engine with high-availability clustering, disaster recovery, vendor support, and additional certified integrations — features not included in the free, self-hosted OSS edition. Ownership context matters for evaluating Conjur's long-term trajectory: CyberArk itself was acquired by Palo Alto Networks in a deal valued near $25 billion that completed in February 2026, meaning Conjur is now positioned within Palo Alto Networks' broader security portfolio rather than as a standalone company's flagship product. Support for the open-source edition is primarily community-driven through the CyberArk Commons Discourse forum rather than a vendor SLA. Conjur is best suited to large enterprises with dedicated security teams, significant compliance requirements, and large volumes of machine identities that need centralized, policy-driven access control, particularly organizations already invested in CyberArk's (now Palo Alto Networks') broader privileged access management (PAM) platform; smaller teams or those wanting a simpler setup often compare Conjur against alternatives like HashiCorp Vault.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Conjur is an open-source secrets management and machine-identity platform acquired by security vendor CyberArk in 2017 and open-sourced later that same year. It provides a policy-based access model, built around a YAML-based language called MAML, for securing secrets and non-human identities used by DevOps pipelines and cloud-native applications.

The server is licensed under LGPL v3.0, with API clients under Apache 2.0, and the codebase is written primarily in Ruby. CyberArk was itself acquired by Palo Alto Networks in a roughly $25 billion deal that closed in February 2026, placing Conjur within a much larger security portfolio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Conjur exposes a REST API for secret storage, retrieval, and identity lifecycle management, with built-in authenticators and rotators that support automatic credential rotation instead of static secrets. Secrets are encrypted with AES-256-GCM via CyberArk's Slosilo library, and multi-tenant account support allows multiple isolated environments in one deployment.

It integrates with major DevOps toolchains — Kubernetes, Docker, Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Terraform, and Jenkins — as well as leading cloud infrastructure platforms, positioning it as infrastructure for machine-to-machine authentication at scale rather than a simple key-value secrets store.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Conjur Open Source is free to download, self-host, and modify under its LGPL v3.0/Apache 2.0 licensing, with community support available through the CyberArk Commons Discourse forum.

CyberArk also sells a commercial product, now branded CyberArk Secrets Manager (formerly Conjur Enterprise), that adds high-availability clustering, disaster recovery, vendor support, and certified integrations on top of the open-source core. Pricing for this commercial tier is custom and quote-based rather than publicly listed.

features.title: Policy-as-Code (MAML)
features.description: A YAML-based language for declaring machines, users, and their access privileges as version-controlled policy.

features.title: REST API for Secrets & Identity
features.description: Programmatic access for storing secrets, managing identity lifecycles, and authorizing resource access.

features.title: Built-In Authenticators & Rotators
features.description: Supports automatic, dynamic credential rotation instead of relying on static, long-lived secrets.

features.title: AES-256-GCM Encryption
features.description: Secrets are encrypted at rest using CyberArk's Slosilo cryptography library.

features.title: Multi-Tenant Account Support
features.description: Multiple isolated environments can be managed from a single Conjur deployment.

features.title: DevOps Toolchain Integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Kubernetes, Docker, Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Terraform, and Jenkins.

features.title: Role-Based Access Policies
features.description: Fine-grained, role-based control over which identities can access which secrets.

pros: Free, open-source core with an enterprise-grade security model
Strong policy-as-code approach to machine identity and access control
Deep integrations across common DevOps and cloud toolchains
Backed by CyberArk's established security expertise
cons: Steeper learning curve (Ruby-based, MAML policy language) than simpler tools like HashiCorp Vault or Doppler
Open-source edition lacks the high-availability clustering and disaster recovery in the commercial tier
Smaller open-source community (under 1,000 GitHub stars) than competing secrets managers
Commercial pricing is quote-based and enterprise-oriented, less accessible to small teams

faqs.question: Is Conjur free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Conjur Open Source is free and self-hosted under LGPL v3.0/Apache 2.0 licenses.

faqs.question: Who owns Conjur?
faqs.answer: CyberArk, which was acquired by Palo Alto Networks in a deal that completed in February 2026.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Conjur Open Source and CyberArk Secrets Manager?
faqs.answer: The commercial tier adds high-availability clustering, disaster recovery, vendor support, and certified integrations on top of the free, open-source engine.

faqs.question: What language is Conjur written in?
faqs.answer: Primarily Ruby, with a YAML-based policy language called MAML for access control.

faqs.question: Does Conjur integrate with Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, along with Docker, Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Terraform, Jenkins, and major cloud platforms.

use_cases.title: Machine Identity & Secrets Management for DevOps Pipelines
use_cases.description: Platform and security teams use Conjur to secure credentials used by CI/CD tools and automated deployments.

use_cases.title: Centralized Secrets Inside a PAM Program
use_cases.description: Large enterprises embed Conjur's policy-driven access model into a broader privileged access management strategy.
use_cases.audience: Large enterprises with dedicated security teams

enrichment.short_description: Connecteam is an all-in-one employee management app combining scheduling, GPS time tracking, task management, HR, and internal communication tools.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 10 users); paid plans from $29/month
enrichment.headquarters: New York, USA and Tel Aviv, Israel
enrichment.seo_title: Connecteam: Employee Management App Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Connecteam is an all-in-one employee management app for deskless teams, covering scheduling, HR, and chat. Free for up to 10 users; paid plans from $29/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Connecteam is an all-in-one employee management app founded in 2014 by Hadar Shmueli and Amir Nehemia, with headquarters listed in both New York City and Tel Aviv, Israel. The product is built specifically for deskless, mobile-first workforces — employees in retail, construction, hospitality, healthcare, and field services who don't sit at a computer with a corporate email account — as an alternative to enterprise HR and workforce-management suites designed around office workers. Connecteam organizes its functionality into three separate 'hubs' that can be purchased independently: an Operations Hub covering GPS-based time clocks, employee scheduling, task management, digital forms and checklists, and geofencing; a Communications Hub covering a company-wide news feed, one-on-one and group chat, scheduled updates, anonymous surveys, and an employee directory; and an HR & Skills Hub covering time-off requests and approvals, employee onboarding, e-signature document management, recognition and reward programs, and training courses. Businesses with up to 10 employees can use a Small Business Plan that is free for life and includes access to all three hubs. Beyond that size, each hub is priced separately on a Basic, Advanced, Expert, or custom Enterprise tier, and pricing is structured as a flat monthly rate covering the first 30 users, with a small per-additional-user fee beyond that threshold — for example, the Operations Hub's Basic tier costs $29/month flat (or $35/month if billed monthly versus annually, with annual billing saving about 18%) for up to 30 users, then roughly $0.80 to $1.00 per additional user per month, while Advanced and Expert tiers cost $49 and $99/month respectively, with correspondingly higher per-user rates. The Communications and HR & Skills hubs follow the same Basic/Advanced/Expert/Enterprise structure at similar flat starting prices but lower per-additional-user costs. Every paid plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required. Feature examples that gate tier upgrades include: on Operations, auto clock-out and expanded geofence site limits on Advanced, and unlimited geofence sites, auto-assigned shifts, and API access on Expert; on Communications, scheduled messages and anonymous surveys on Advanced, and API access with auto-translated updates on Expert; on HR & Skills, e-signature document limits that expand from Advanced to Expert, alongside unlimited training courses starting at Advanced. Connecteam offers dedicated iOS and Android mobile apps designed around frontline and non-desk employees rather than a desktop-first experience. It is positioned as a lower-cost, mobile-native alternative to larger enterprise HR platforms for small and mid-sized businesses that primarily manage hourly or shift-based workforces.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Connecteam is an all-in-one employee management app founded in 2014 by Hadar Shmueli and Amir Nehemia, headquartered in both New York City and Tel Aviv, Israel. It is built specifically for deskless, mobile-first workforces — retail, construction, hospitality, healthcare, and field service employees — rather than office workers with corporate email accounts.

The product is organized into three purchasable hubs: Operations (time tracking, scheduling, tasks), Communications (company chat and updates), and HR & Skills (time off, onboarding, training, recognition), which businesses can mix and match based on their needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Operations Hub includes GPS-based time clocks, employee scheduling, task management, digital forms and checklists, and geofencing for job sites. The Communications Hub provides a company-wide news feed, one-on-one and group chat, scheduled updates, anonymous surveys, and an employee directory.

The HR & Skills Hub covers time-off requests and approvals, onboarding, e-signature document management, employee recognition and rewards, and training courses. Dedicated iOS and Android apps are designed around frontline, non-desk employees rather than a desktop-first workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Businesses with up to 10 employees can use the Small Business Plan free for life, with access to all three hubs. Beyond that, each hub is sold separately on Basic, Advanced, Expert, or custom Enterprise tiers, priced as a flat rate covering the first 30 users (for example, Operations Hub Basic starts at $29/month) plus a small per-additional-user fee beyond 30 users.
Annual billing saves roughly 18% compared to monthly billing, and every paid plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

features.title: GPS Time Clock & Scheduling
features.description: Employees clock in and out with location tracking, and managers build and adjust shift schedules.

features.title: Task Management & Checklists
features.description: Digital forms, checklists, and task assignments replace paper-based processes.

features.title: Company-Wide Chat & Announcements
features.description: A news feed plus one-on-one and group chat keep deskless teams connected.

features.title: Time-Off & HR Document Management
features.description: Employees request time off and sign HR documents electronically.

features.title: Employee Training & Onboarding Courses
features.description: Build and assign training courses directly within the app.

features.title: Recognition & Rewards
features.description: Managers can recognize employees with badges and rewards.

features.title: Geofencing
features.description: Restrict clock-ins to defined job-site locations.

features.title: API Access
features.description: Available on Expert-tier plans for integrating with other systems.

pros: Free for life for businesses with up to 10 employees
Purpose-built for deskless and mobile-first workforces
Modular hub pricing lets teams pay only for the features they need
14-day free trial with no credit card required
cons: Pricing across three separate hubs can add up if a team needs all of them
Feature limits within each tier (like geofence site counts) require upgrades to remove
Less depth in payroll and benefits administration than dedicated HR platforms
API access and some advanced features are gated behind Expert or Enterprise tiers

faqs.question: Is Connecteam free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Small Business Plan is free for life for businesses with up to 10 employees, including access to all three hubs.

faqs.question: Who founded Connecteam?
faqs.answer: Hadar Shmueli and Amir Nehemia founded Connecteam in 2014.

faqs.question: How is Connecteam priced?
faqs.answer: Separately by hub (Operations, Communications, HR & Skills), each on Basic/Advanced/Expert/Enterprise tiers, with a flat rate for the first 30 users and a small per-additional-user fee beyond that.

faqs.question: Does Connecteam have a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 14-day free trial with no credit card required is available on paid plans.

faqs.question: Is Connecteam available on mobile?
faqs.answer: Yes, dedicated iOS and Android apps are built specifically for frontline, deskless employees.

enrichment.short_description: Constant Contact is an email and SMS marketing platform for small businesses, offering automation, landing pages, and AI-assisted content tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Constant Contact: Email Marketing Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Constant Contact is an email and SMS marketing platform for small businesses. Plans start at $12/month for 500 contacts, with automation and landing pages.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Constant Contact is an email and SMS marketing platform originally founded in 1995 as Roving Software by Randy Parker; the company adopted the Constant Contact name in 2004 and went public in 2007, making it one of the longest-running email marketing platforms still operating today. It was acquired by Endurance International Group (EIG) in 2016, and following a period under EIG's ownership of multiple web/marketing brands, it now operates under Clearlake Capital ownership. The company is headquartered in Waltham, Massachusetts, with additional US offices in Boston, Loveland (Colorado), Gainesville (Florida), and Los Angeles, plus international offices in Brisbane (Australia), Waterloo (Canada), and London (UK). Constant Contact is positioned primarily for small businesses and nonprofit organizations that are new to digital marketing, emphasizing ease of use and customer support over the deeper technical customization found in developer-oriented platforms. Core functionality includes a drag-and-drop email builder, AI-assisted content and subject-line generation, marketing automation for common small-business scenarios such as welcome series, abandoned-cart reminders, re-engagement campaigns, birthday messages, and post-purchase follow-ups, SMS/text marketing that can be coordinated alongside email sends, landing pages and website sign-up forms or pop-ups for lead capture, social media post scheduling, and (on higher tiers) Google and Facebook ad management plus basic SEO tools. As of 2026, Constant Contact's pricing is structured in three tiers, all starting at a base of 500 contacts: Lite at $12/month, which includes one user, core email sending, and a single automation template; Standard at $35/month, which adds up to three users, full marketing automation, A/B testing, and social media scheduling; and Premium at $80/month, which includes unlimited users, advanced automation, Google and Facebook ad management, and SEO tools. Pricing scales with contact list size rather than staying fixed — for example, published estimates put a 2,500-contact Standard plan around $70/month, while a 5,001-10,000 contact range costs roughly $120/month (Lite), $160/month (Standard), and $275/month (Premium). Constant Contact does not offer a permanent free plan; instead it offers a free trial period before billing begins. Its primary competitors in the small-business and nonprofit email marketing space include Mailchimp, ActiveCampaign, and Campaign Monitor, with Constant Contact generally differentiated by its long operating history, extensive live customer support options, and simplicity aimed at users without prior email marketing or design experience, rather than by advanced segmentation or automation depth.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Constant Contact traces back to 1995, when it was founded as Roving Software by Randy Parker; it took the Constant Contact name in 2004 and went public in 2007, making it one of the longest-running email marketing platforms still in operation. The company is headquartered in Waltham, Massachusetts, with additional offices across the US, Australia, Canada, and the UK.

It is positioned primarily for small businesses and nonprofits that are new to digital marketing, prioritizing ease of use and customer support over deep technical customization, and now operates under Clearlake Capital ownership after being acquired by Endurance International Group in 2016.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes a drag-and-drop email builder, AI-assisted content and subject-line generation, and marketing automation for common scenarios like welcome series, abandoned-cart reminders, re-engagement campaigns, and post-purchase follow-ups.

The platform also includes SMS/text marketing coordinated with email campaigns, landing pages and sign-up forms for lead capture, social media post scheduling, and, on higher tiers, Google and Facebook ad management alongside basic SEO tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Constant Contact's 2026 pricing has three tiers starting at a 500-contact base: Lite at $12/month, Standard at $35/month, and Premium at $80/month, each adding more users, automation, and marketing channels at higher tiers.

Pricing scales with contact list size rather than staying flat — for example, a 5,001-10,000 contact range costs roughly $120/month on Lite up to $275/month on Premium — and there is no permanent free plan, only a free trial period before billing begins.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Email Builder
features.description: Build branded email campaigns without design or coding experience.

features.title: Marketing Automation
features.description: Automated welcome series, abandoned-cart reminders, re-engagement, and post-purchase campaigns.

features.title: SMS/Text Marketing
features.description: Coordinate text message campaigns alongside email sends.

features.title: Landing Pages & Sign-Up Forms
features.description: Build lead-capture landing pages and website sign-up forms or pop-ups.

features.title: AI-Assisted Content Generation
features.description: AI tools help draft email copy and subject lines.

features.title: Social Media Posting & Ad Management
features.description: Schedule social posts and, on higher tiers, manage Google and Facebook ad campaigns.

features.title: A/B Testing
features.description: Test subject lines and content variations on Standard and higher plans.

features.title: Reporting & Analytics
features.description: Track open rates, click-throughs, and campaign performance.

pros: Easy-to-use interface aimed at marketing beginners
Combines email, SMS, and social media scheduling in one platform
Long operating history (founded 1995) with extensive customer support options
Automation covers common small-business scenarios out of the box
cons: No permanent free plan, only a free trial
Pricing scales quickly as a contact list grows
Fewer advanced segmentation and automation options than competitors like ActiveCampaign
Templates and interface viewed by some reviewers as less modern than newer entrants

faqs.question: Does Constant Contact have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No permanent free plan; it offers a free trial period before billing begins.

faqs.question: How much does Constant Contact cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start around $12/month (Lite) for 500 contacts, scaling up with plan tier (Standard $35/month, Premium $80/month) and contact list size.

faqs.question: When was Constant Contact founded?
faqs.answer: It was founded in 1995 as Roving Software and took the Constant Contact name in 2004.

faqs.question: Does Constant Contact support SMS marketing?
faqs.answer: Yes, text marketing is available alongside email campaigns.

faqs.question: Who owns Constant Contact?
faqs.answer: It currently operates under Clearlake Capital ownership, after previously being part of Endurance International Group, which acquired it in 2016.

use_cases.title: Email Newsletters and Promotions
use_cases.description: Small business owners send regular newsletters and promotional campaigns to build customer relationships.
